claim: CMS finalized (7 Apr 2022) that Medicare covers anti-amyloid Alzheimer's antibodies only under Coverage with Evidence Development (CED) - a registry condition that gated patient access to Leqembi/Aduhelm and the whole class.
bill_rule: CMS National Coverage Determination - Monoclonal Antibodies Directed Against Amyloid for AD (Coverage with Evidence Development)
status: finalized 2022-04-07; registry-based coverage for traditionally-approved drugs from 2023 (Leqembi)
program_impact: Access/pricing headwind for the entire anti-amyloid CNS class: Medicare reimbursement conditioned on CMS-approved registry participation, slowing real-world uptake of lecanemab/donanemab.
quote: Medicare will cover monoclonal antibodies that target amyloid (or plaque) for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease that receive traditional approval from the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) under coverage with evidence development (CED).

claim: CMS selected 15 drugs for the 2nd cycle of Medicare Drug Price Negotiation (announced 17 Jan 2025; maximum fair prices effective 2027), including two CNS medicines - Vraylar (cariprazine; schizophrenia/bipolar) and Austedo (deutetrabenazine; Huntington's chorea / tardive dyskinesia).
bill_rule: Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program (IRA) - second-cycle selected-drug list (IPAY 2027)
status: selected 2025-01-17; negotiated maximum fair prices effective 2027
program_impact: Direct US price pressure on marketed CNS franchises once 7-yr small-molecule eligibility is met; signals CNS drugs are squarely in scope of Medicare price negotiation.
quote:
Austedo; Austedo XR 
Breo Ellipta 
Tradjenta
Xifaxan
Vraylar

claim: FDA's accelerated-approval program - reformed by FDORA (2022), e.g. the Section 3210 Accelerated Approval Coordinating Council - requires confirmatory trials to verify clinical benefit; the framework tightened after CNS controversy (the aducanumab accelerated approval).
bill_rule: Food and Drug Omnibus Reform Act (FDORA) 2022, accelerated-approval reforms (Consolidated Appropriations Act 2023, Public Law 117-328)
status: enacted 2022-12-29; FDA implementing (Coordinating Council; 2025 'confirmatory trial underway' draft guidance)
program_impact: Raises the bar for surrogate-based CNS accelerated approvals - confirmatory trials expected underway at approval; expedited withdrawal if benefit unconfirmed.
quote: Drug companies are still required to conduct studies to confirm the anticipated clinical benefit. ... Report covers 2025 activities required by Section 3210 of FDORA

claim: Under the IRA, small-molecule drugs become eligible for Medicare price negotiation 7 years after FDA approval vs 11 years for biologics (the '4-year pill penalty') - a structural disincentive for small-molecule R&D that disproportionately affects CNS, which is overwhelmingly small-molecule.
bill_rule: Inflation Reduction Act (P.L. 117-169) - Drug Price Negotiation eligibility timing; EPIC Act (H.R. 1492) proposed fix
program_impact: Shorter pre-negotiation window for small molecules is cited as reducing early-stage small-molecule investment - a headwind for CNS small-molecule programs specifically.
quote: high-spending drugs can be selected for negotiation if they are brand-name drugs or biological products without generic or biosimilar equivalents, and at least 7 years (for small molecule drugs) or 11 years (for biologics) past their FDA approval or licensure date

claim: Lecanemab's CLARITY AD benefit was modest - CDR-SB worsened 1.21 (drug) vs 1.66 (placebo), a -0.45-point difference over 18 months (~27% relative slowing) - the small absolute effect that drives the coverage/cost-effectiveness policy debate (CMS CED; NICE refusal).
program_impact: The modest effect magnitude is the biomedical basis for coverage-with-evidence-development and HTA non-reimbursement of anti-amyloid antibodies.
quote: The adjusted least-squares mean change from baseline at 18 months was 1.21 with lecanemab and 1.66 with placebo (difference, -0.45; 95% confidence interval [CI], -0.67 to -0.23; P<0.001).

claim: Clinician-anchored minimal clinically important differences (MCIDs) for CDR-SB (~0.98 for MCI, 1.63 for mild dementia) far exceed lecanemab's 0.45-point effect - the benchmark gap underpinning payers'/HTAs' 'benefit too small for the cost' coverage decisions.
program_impact: Quantifies why coverage bodies judge the anti-amyloid effect sub-threshold; the MCID-vs-observed-effect gap is the crux of CED and NICE/EMA scrutiny.
quote: The often referenced MCID for mild cognitive impairment (0.98) thereby corresponded to 11 months slowing, whereas the MCID for mild dementia (1.63) corresponded to >17 months slowing.
